title: Tidy First overview and map
summary: The graph is organized around code-tidying practices, management decisions, and theory that explains why small structural changes can be valuable.

title: Early local tidies
summary: Guard clauses, dead-code deletion, symmetry, new interfaces, and reading order are early moves for making code easier to read before larger changes.

title: Locality and naming tidies
summary: Cohesion layout, declaration placement, explaining variables, explaining constants, and explicit parameters make intent and data flow easier to inspect.

title: Extraction and comment tidies
summary: Similar-code grouping, helper extraction, one-pile collection, explaining comments, and redundant-comment deletion manage duplication and reader guidance.

title: Boundaries and chained tidying
summary: Tidying should remain distinguishable from behavior change, and chained tidies should be controlled by the current engineering goal.

title: Batch size, rhythm, untangling, and timing
summary: Management guidance covers how much to tidy at once, how to keep a steady rhythm, how to untangle mixed edits, and when tidying is worth doing.

title: Structure, behavior, and economics
summary: The theory section frames software as elements and relations, separates structure from behavior, and evaluates tidying through economic tradeoffs.

title: Options and reversibility
summary: Options thinking values small reversible structure changes when they preserve future choices under uncertainty.

title: Coupling, decoupling, and cohesion
summary: Coupling explains change cost, decoupling must be economically justified, and cohesion guides where related elements should live.
